Check Address Report Scam Scam Database WALLET ADDRESS OR WEBSITE URL Check WARNING: 1 scam report found TYPE: PHISHING LOST: ₦250,000 DATE: 15 MAR 2026 PLATFORM: FAKEYIELD AFRICA Victim was asked to connect wallet to a fake exchange clone. 10 Signs a Crypto Opportunity is a Scam 1 💰 Guaranteed returns "Earn 300% in 30 days" — no legitimate investment can guarantee returns. 2 ⏱ Pressure to act fast "Only 10 spots left!" — urgency is a classic manipulation tactic. 3 🕵 Unverifiable team No LinkedIn profiles, no public history, stock photos for "team members." 4 📄 No whitepaper or clear product Legitimate projects explain their technology. Scams only talk about money. 5 📤 Asks you to send crypto to a "trading expert" No real trader needs your coins in their wallet. 6 ⭐ Claims of celebrity endorsement Fake quotes from Elon Musk, Dangote, or local celebrities are a huge red flag. 7 📱 Telegram/WhatsApp-only communication Real companies have websites, support emails, and registered offices. 8 🔒 Requests your seed phrase or pri

Crypto Education in Africa: Why Knowledge Matters Africa is one of the fastest-growing cryptocurrency markets in the world, with countries like Nigeria, Kenya, South Africa, Ghana, and Egypt leading adoption on the continent. According to industry reports, Africa's share of global crypto transaction volume has grown steadily year over year, driven by a young, tech-savvy population seeking alternatives to traditional banking systems that often fail to serve them. Peer-to-peer trading platforms have seen explosive growth, with stablecoins like USDT becoming essential tools for cross-border commerce, remittances, and protection against local currency depreciation. Despite this rapid adoption, crypto education in Africa remains uneven. Many new users enter the market through social media recommendations or community groups without understanding fundamental concepts like private key security, the difference between custod
